text: The growth behavior of carbon dots (CDs) is a concern at the molecular level, which is still controversial, hampering the fast development of CDs research. In the present work, highly fluorescent blue nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) are synthesized in situ by means of a one-step hydrothermal strategy based on 4-aminoantipyrine at different growth times ranging from 3 to 21 h. Through FT-IR, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and UV-vis spectroscopy, the evolution behavior and forming mechanism are explored in detail. The results demonstrated that the increase of hydrothermal time endows precursor more aromatic and noncovalent π–π interaction, meanwhile breaking the pyrazole ring at the resulting NCDs are amorphous in structure with a particle size of about 5 nm in diameter with an increasing carbon core being coated progressively with polar aliphatic groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine. Consequently, the fluorescence quantum yield increases with the increasing growth time. Structure and morphology are characterized by HR-TEM and XRD, showing good aqueous stability, and exhibiting a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm. In summary, the whole growing mechanism was explored precisely, and an economic method was applied to synthesize highly fluorescent blue NCDs efficiently which can have great potential in the fields of material science and biomedicine.
